misconceptions.claim: pointwise continuity on an open interval ⟹ uniform continuity
misconceptions.counterexample: f(x) = 1/x is continuous on (0,1) but NOT uniformly continuous: for any δ > 0 and small x near 0, |f(x) - f(x+δ/2)| can exceed any fixed ε.
misconceptions.explanation: Uniform continuity requires one δ to work for all pairs of points. On an open (non-compact) interval, the function can 'speed up' near the boundary, breaking uniform continuity.

misconceptions.counterexample: f(x) = √x on [0,1] is uniformly continuous (compact domain, continuous) but NOT Lipschitz at 0: |f(x) - f(0)| / |x - 0| = 1/√x → ∞ as x → 0⁺.
misconceptions.explanation: Uniform continuity is weaker than Lipschitz continuity. A Lipschitz constant bounds the ratio Δf/Δx; uniform continuity only bounds Δf given Δx → 0 uniformly, with no rate requirement.

misconceptions.claim: continuous on a compact set does NOT imply uniform continuity — you still need to check the ε-δ condition globally
misconceptions.counterexample: Any continuous f: [a,b] → ℝ is uniformly continuous — this is exactly Cantor's Theorem (also called the Heine-Cantor theorem). No additional ε-δ verification is needed.
misconceptions.explanation: Cantor's Theorem guarantees that continuity on a compact metric space automatically upgrades to uniform continuity. This is one of the most powerful uses of compactness in analysis.
